title: Listening - Part 2
description: You will hear an archeologist called Oliver Grant talking about his work excavating mountain sites. For questions 7 – 14, complete the sentences with a word or short phrase.
instructions: You will hear an archeologist called Oliver Grant talking about his work excavating mountain sites. For questions 7 – 14, complete the sentences with a word or short phrase.

extracts.context: Oliver Grant discusses the technical and physical challenges of discovering artifacts in extreme environments.
extracts.audio_script:
Hello, I’m Oliver. Most archeology happens in warm, dusty places, but I prefer the cold. I specialize in high-altitude archeology. My interest in this field didn't start in a museum; it actually began when I found a [7]broken flint arrowhead[/7] while hiking in the Alps as a teenager. I was at three thousand meters, and it amazed me that someone had been there thousands of years before me. I wanted to know what they were doing so high up.

Excavating in the mountains is a race against time. Because of global warming, glaciers are melting and revealing artifacts that have been frozen for millennia. I’ve found that the [8]speed of decay[/8] is our biggest concern. Once an organic object—like a piece of leather or wood—melts out of the ice, it can disintegrate in just a few days if it’s not properly preserved. We have to be ready to act the moment something is discovered.

The physical demands are extreme. We often work in thin air, which makes even small tasks exhausting. I’ve found that [9]solar-powered freezers[/9] are the most important piece of equipment we carry. We need to keep the artifacts frozen from the moment we find them until they reach the lab. If they warm up during transport, the cellular structure collapses and we lose vital information. It’s a logistical nightmare.

We are finding things that change our understanding of history. Last year, I found a [10]woven grass cloak[/10] that was five thousand years old. The level of detail in the stitching was incredible. It showed that ancient people had a much more sophisticated understanding of thermal insulation than we previously gave them credit for. They weren't just surviving the cold; they were mastering it.

Technology is helping us narrow our search. We use satellite imagery to identify 'ice patches' that are likely to contain artifacts. I’ve noticed that [11]dark-colored rocks[/11] are a good indicator of where to look. They absorb more heat, which causes the surrounding ice to melt faster, often revealing objects buried underneath. It’s a simple observation that has saved us weeks of aimless searching.

Working in such an environment requires a unique set of skills. You have to be an expert climber as well as a scientist. I’ve found that the [12]sudden weather shifts[/12] are the most dangerous part of the job. You can be working in bright sunshine one minute and be in a life-threatening blizzard the next. You have to be able to pack up your site and find shelter in seconds.

Looking back, the biggest change in the field is the involvement of local communities. I believe that [13]indigenous knowledge[/13] is just as important as our scientific data. Local hunters and shepherds often know where the ice is melting and where ancient trails used to be. By working together, we can protect these sites before they are looted or destroyed.

Finally, I’m currently developing a [14]digital archive[/14] that will allow people to see 3D models of our finds from their own homes. I want to share the wonder of high-altitude discovery with the world without putting the fragile artifacts at risk. Thank you.
